title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

texts.A: Virtual reality represents what may genuinely be described as the ultimate empathy machine for education. Consider the transformative potential: a history student genuinely 'standing' in a muddy trench during the First World War and hearing the sounds of battle, or a biology student 'shrinking' to molecular scale and physically navigating the interior of a living human cell. [38]This extraordinary level of full sensory immersion creates a lasting and deeply personal emotional and intellectual connection to subject matter that conventional textbooks, however well written, can simply never hope to replicate or approach.[/38] Critics dismiss VR as an expensive distraction, but identical arguments were confidently made against the introduction of both the internet and the electronic calculator into classrooms, and history has comprehensively refuted both predictions. VR is not designed to replace the teacher but to provide them with an unprecedented superpower: the ability to make the genuinely abstract and remote viscerally tangible, memorable, and educationally unforgettable for every single learner.
texts.B: [39]The capital and ongoing operational cost of implementing VR technology at meaningful and genuinely equitable scale across an entire national school system is prohibitive and would almost certainly and significantly deepen the existing digital divide between well-resourced schools serving affluent communities and chronically underfunded schools in disadvantaged areas that most need innovative educational interventions.[/39] Furthermore, [37]I share the serious and growing concern that an excessive and normalized reliance on gamified and visually spectacular learning experiences progressively and measurably erodes the student fundamental cognitive capacity and willingness to sustain focused attention on demanding, non-visual intellectual tasks such as extended analytical reading of complex texts and formal abstract logical reasoning requiring patience and mental discipline.[/37] The fundamental purpose of education should be the systematic training of the mind to process genuinely complex information with rigor, patience, and critical independence, not merely providing an expensive high-tech spectacle that substitutes passive audiovisual stimulation for genuine intellectual development. We risk raising an entire generation unable to learn without constant digital entertainment.
texts.C: I agree that VR technology holds genuine and remarkable potential as a transformative tool for inherently visual and spatial subjects, but I share the broader concern that extensive and unrestricted classroom use raises serious and underappreciated questions about the quality of children's social and emotional development. [40]If students are required to wear fully immersive VR headsets for extended periods throughout the school day, they are being systematically denied the crucial practice of direct peer-to-peer eye contact, real-time emotional reading, and the interpretation of subtle body language and facial expression that are absolutely foundational to developing robust and genuine emotional intelligence.[/40] However, I firmly disagree with the reductive characterization of VR as mere 'entertainment technology.' [38]When deployed thoughtfully and with appropriate pedagogical design, VR has a particularly significant and evidence-based potential to genuinely assist students with neurodivergent needs by offering a precisely controllable, adjustable, sensory-friendly learning environment that a conventional and often overwhelming traditional classroom structure fundamentally cannot provide.[/38]
texts.D: The most transformative and genuinely significant potential of virtual reality in education lies not in the provision of spectacular individual 'experiences' or immersive historical reconstructions, but in enabling unprecedented forms of genuine cross-cultural collaboration and shared intellectual inquiry. [40]VR uniquely enables students from entirely different parts of the world—with different languages, cultures, and perspectives—to work together simultaneously in a shared virtual space, breaking down geographical, cultural, and socioeconomic barriers that have historically made true international educational collaboration impractical.[/40] [39]While I share the legitimate concern about the significant current cost of hardware and implementation, I am confident that prices will fall substantially and rapidly as the technology matures and economies of scale develop, as has occurred consistently with every previous consumer technology.[/39] [37]I fundamentally disagree with the claim that VR inherently hinders the development of abstract thought; on the contrary, it specifically enables students to directly visualize and manipulate the abstract.[/37] A student who can 'see' and interact with a four-dimensional mathematical space is demonstrably more likely to develop genuine intuitive understanding of the underlying theory.

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from B on whether VR hinders the understanding of abstract concepts?
questions.explanation: Expert B thinks it reduces focus on 'abstract logic.' Expert D argues it actually helps students 'visualize the abstract' and understand the theory behind it.

questions.question: Which expert shares A's opinion about the unique 'immersion' and emotional power of VR?
questions.explanation: Expert A calls VR an 'empathy machine' with 'lasting emotional connection.' Expert C also agrees it is an 'incredible tool' that provides a unique 'sensory-friendly environment.'

questions.question: Which expert expresses a similar view to B regarding the financial barriers to VR education?
questions.explanation: Expert B highlights the 'prohibitive cost' and the 'digital divide.' Expert D also 'shares the concern about the cost,' although they are more optimistic about future prices.

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from C on the social impact of using VR in schools?
questions.explanation: Expert C fears a loss of 'peer-to-peer eye contact' and social development. Expert D argues it actually fosters 'collaboration' and allows students to work together globally.
